title: Full Extractors for Logical Processing in Hypergraph Product Codes
authors: John Blue, Zhiyang He, Hengyun Zhou, Isaac L. Chuang
abstract: Quantum low-density parity-check (QLDPC) codes are promising candidates for practical low-overhead quantum memories. For large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computation, we further need logical processing methods for QLDPC codes. In this work, we construct full extractors -- surgery systems capable of measuring arbitrary logical Pauli operators on a code block -- for several hypergraph product (HGP) codes. These extractors enable logical processing via Pauli-based computation (PBC) without the compilation overhead observed in prior works. Moreover, our extractors have sizes between 50% and 80% of the base HGP codes, and the extractor-augmented codes can be supported on fixed connectivity hardware with maximum qubit degree ten. Our approach involves assembling many partial extractors with verifiable fault-tolerance into a single full extractor. For a distance 10 HGP code, circuit-level noise simulations yield logical measurement error rates of approximately $10^{-6}$ at a physical error rate of 0.1%. These results demonstrate that extractor architectures, when designed in the fixed-connectivity setting, can achieve the space efficiency of QLDPC codes without introducing compilation overhead compared to surface code PBC architectures.
summary: This paper introduces 'full extractors' designed to enable arbitrary logical Pauli measurements on Hypergraph Product (HGP) quantum codes. By assembling partial extractors, the authors demonstrate a method that maintains high space efficiency and low, fixed connectivity, crucial for scaling fault-tolerant quantum computers. The results show that this architecture achieves low logical error rates comparable to the base code without the overhead penalties of prior methods.
main_problem: The paper addresses the need for practical, low-overhead logical processing methods for Quantum Low-Density Parity-Check (QLDPC) codes required for large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computation.
main_result: They construct 'full extractors' for Hypergraph Product (HGP) codes that achieve space efficiencies of 50-80% of the base code while supporting fixed, low-degree connectivity and avoiding compilation overhead.
method: The methodology involves assembling full extractors by connecting multiple verifiable partial extractors, utilizing graph theory and algebraic structures derived from the HGP code's homology/cohomology groups.
model_or_system: The system analyzed is the Hypergraph Product (HGP) code, a type of CSS QLDPC code. The core components are the base HGP code, the X-basis extractor, the Z-basis extractor, and the resulting full extractor structure.
key_observables: Logical measurement error rates (e.g., $\approx 10^{-6}$ at $p=0.1\%$), space overhead (50-80% of base code), and maximum qubit degree (ten).
important_parameters: Physical error rate ($p$), code distance ($d=10$), and the number of merged rounds ($N_R$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the existence of distance-preserving single-column extractors and relies on the ability to assemble these partial extractors into a full system.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the structure of HGP codes, the construction of Z and X extractors, and the final full extractor connecting these components. Figure 9 compares logical measurement error rates between different decoding schemes.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the need for logical processing in QLDPC codes, then detailing the construction of single-column and basis extractors using graph theory and algebraic invariants. It culminates in proving the fault tolerance and efficiency of the full extractor, followed by performance comparisons with existing decoding methods.
why_interesting: This work provides concrete, resource-aware architectural blueprints for implementing complex logical operations on near-term quantum hardware by optimizing the overhead associated with fault-tolerance.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) An HGP code Q with example logical X and Z operators highlighted. Note that the layout of the HGP code is purely for visualization purposes and does not correspond to a physical layout. The cyclic HGP code depicted uses the check matrix H which is the binary circulant matrix corresponding to h(x) = 1 + x2 and is a [[72, 4, 3]] code. (b) A single-column extractor XC used to measure logical Z operators supported on the first column of the left block. The activated connections between the extractor and the first column used to measure the shown logical Z operator are highlighted in pink, and the inactive connections are shown in light gray. (c) Example supports of a vertex check...

FIG. 2. (a) A Z extractor (XZ), capable of measuring any logical Z operator on the base code Q. The Z extractor is formed by connecting k single-column extractors with bridges. Lines between the extractor and base code show how they are connected, e.g. the vertex checks of the first column of XZ are connected to the first column of the left block and the first row of the right block of Q. The connections between XZ and the rows of the right block are governed by the permutation σ. (b) Connecting an X extractor (XX) and a Z extractor (XZ) with a bridge B to form a full extractor.

FIG. 3. Logical error rates of the [[882, 50, 10]] HGP code and extractor system used to measure a randomly sampled element of {I, X, Y, Z}⊗50. For the extractor system, “Meas” refers to the error rate of the logical measurement, and “Block” refers to the logical error rate of the other 49 logical qubits during the experiment. Note that the shown logical error rates are not normalized per round or per logical qubit. Error bars indicate 95% Agresti-Coull confidence intervals. (a) A comparison of the logical error rates for the base HGP code, and the HGP+extractor system. For the base code, the y axis shows the logical error of a Z-basis memory experiment with sixteen rounds of syndrome...

FIG. 4. Canonical basis of logical operators for HGP(∂A, ∂B), illustrated on a small representative instance (nA=nB=6, mA=mB=4). Each logical qubit (i, j) is associated with an information qubit at which its paired ¯Z and ¯ X operators intersect. On the left block, ¯ZL ij occupies information column j (sparse, along the codeword cA,i ∈ker ∂A) while ¯ XL ij occupies information row i (sparse, along cB,j ∈ker ∂B). On the right block the roles of rows and columns are exchanged, with codewords drawn from the transpose codes ker ∂⊤ A and ker ∂⊤ B. Light shading indicates the information rows/columns; the unique ¯Z– ¯ X intersection (violet) is the information qubit.

FIG. 5. Structure of code surgery and its constituent definitions (Appendix E). An ancilla system (A, f, D) consists of a CSS ancilla code A (drawn as a graph: vertices = AZ, edges = ancilla qubits, cycles = AX) together with a port function f : supp(P) →AZ satisfying im(f) ⊂supp(c), where c is the unique nonzero element of ker H⊤ Z , and a deformation map D : S →P(E) assigning each base-code stabilizer a path matching of K(S) in the ancilla graph G. Logical measurement is performed by measuring the stabilizers of the deformed code Q′ whose checks can be classified into three groups: vertex checks (each Z-check of A augmented by the Paulis L(q) for code qubits mapped to it by f); cycle...
